clusters.title: The Schools Giving Children a Say in How They Are Run
clusters.summary: When a six-year-old suggests a "buddy bench" for lonely classmates and watches it become real, something shifts - not just in the schoolyard, but in how that child understands their own place in the world. Smart School Councils, a UK charity founded by former teacher Greg Sanderson, has built a simple platform that gives even the youngest pupils a structured voice in shaping their school life, with nearly 90% of participating students now reporting that they feel listened to. The premise is modest but the stakes are not: as trust in democratic institutions erodes and young people grow increasingly disenchanted with politics, Sanderson believes that civic confidence must be cultivated long before anyone is old enough to vote.

items.title: The Schools Giving Children a Say in How They Are Run
items.description: When a six-year-old suggests a "buddy bench" for lonely classmates and watches it become real, something shifts - not just in the schoolyard, but in how that child understands their own place in the world. Smart School Councils, a UK charity founded by former teacher Greg Sanderson, has built a simple platform that gives even the youngest pupils a structured voice in shaping their school life, with nearly 90% of participating students now reporting that they feel listened to. The premise is modest but the stakes are not: as trust in democratic institutions erodes and young people grow increasingly disenchanted with politics, Sanderson believes that civic confidence must be cultivated long before anyone is old enough to vote. As assistant headteacher Ellie Nott puts it, "If you can look back and say, 'I had an idea when I was six, and somebody listened, and then it happened', that will stand children in good stead, hopefully, to vote when they're older." What the project quietly reveals is that democracy doesn't fail first at the ballot box - it fails when people never learn, in their bones, that their voice can change anything at all.

items.title: The schools giving children a say in how they are run

As trust in democracy declines, a project putting pupils’ voices at the heart of school life is proving its worth

clusters.title: Mister Rogers Stamps to Be Reissued by USPS After Contest Win
clusters.summary: The United States Postal Service has announced the reissuance of the popular Mister Rogers stamps following their victory in a recent contest. These stamps, celebrating the beloved children's television icon, will be available for purchase starting in June. The decision reflects ongoing public affection for Mister Rogers and his impact on generations of viewers.

items.title: Mister Rogers Stamp Wins USPS Contest, Will Now Be Reissued
Beloved Mister Rogers stamps reissued by USPS after winning 'encore contest'
items.description: The beloved “Mister Rogers’ Neighborhood” stamps will be available to purchase in June.

clusters.title: A Pangolin Pup Found in a Cardboard Box
clusters.summary: Inside a car boot in Johannesburg, a three-month-old pangolin pup lay curled in a cardboard box, a cabbage leaf beside him — the trafficker's well-meaning but useless gesture toward feeding an animal whose diet consists entirely of ants and termites. That detail somehow says everything about the vast distance between the illegal wildlife trade and the natural world it plunders. Named Stevie, after the police officer whose sting operation saved him, the pup spent six months in the careful hands of veterinarian Kelsey Skinner — bottle-fed cat milk formula, walked daily through natural habitat where she'd flip rocks to expose ant eggs, slowly coaxed back toward wildness.

items.title: A Pangolin Pup Found in a Cardboard Box
items.description: Inside a car boot in Johannesburg, a three-month-old pangolin pup lay curled in a cardboard box, a cabbage leaf beside him — the trafficker's well-meaning but useless gesture toward feeding an animal whose diet consists entirely of ants and termites. That detail somehow says everything about the vast distance between the illegal wildlife trade and the natural world it plunders. Named Stevie, after the police officer whose sting operation saved him, the pup spent six months in the careful hands of veterinarian Kelsey Skinner — bottle-fed cat milk formula, walked daily through natural habitat where she'd flip rocks to expose ant eggs, slowly coaxed back toward wildness. Pangolins are the world's most trafficked wild mammal, their scales ground into traditional medicine, their meat sold as delicacy, yet they are so poorly understood that the first rescuer to encounter one in 2008 simply had to "release it back into a safe habitat and hope for the best." What's been built since then — the expertise, the facilities, the networks — is a quiet, painstaking science of return. Stevie was eventually released into the Manyoni Private Game Reserve, fitted with telemetry trackers, and left to find his own way. The trackers now show him regularly visiting females in their burrows. "It's pretty guaranteed," says Skinner, "that he is now a father to a few pangolin pups." A cabbage leaf in a cardboard box to fatherhood in the Zululand bush — the distance traveled in that arc is remarkable.
